---
title: "murkml — Cross-Site SSC Prediction"
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import json
from pathlib import Path

data_dir = Path("dashboard_data")

# Load summary
with open(data_dir / "summary.json") as f:
    summary = json.load(f)
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
## One model, 254 sites, Bayesian adaptation

murkml predicts suspended sediment concentration (SSC) from turbidity using a single CatBoost model trained across 254 USGS sites. Rather than fitting a separate regression at each site, a shared model learns cross-site patterns, then Bayesian adaptation with just 10 local grab samples achieves **MedSiteR² = 0.492** — beating USGS OLS regressions that require 50+ samples.
:::

## Key Numbers

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Training sites | 254 |
| Holdout sites | 76 (never seen in training) |
| Features | 72 (137 in tier, 65 dropped) |
| Zero-shot MedSiteR² | 0.409 (bcf_mean) |
| Adapted (N=10) MedSiteR² | 0.492 |
| External Spearman (260 NTU sites) | 0.927 |

## Explore the Dashboard

- **[Performance](performance.qmd)** — Observed vs predicted plots, pooled vs per-site metrics, and error structure across the prediction range.
- **[Adaptation](adaptation.qmd)** — How Bayesian adaptation improves site-level accuracy with increasing local sample counts.
- **[Sites](sites.qmd)** — Individual site performance breakdowns.
- **[External](external.qmd)** — Validation against 260 independent NTU monitoring sites.
- **[Diagnostics](diagnostics.qmd)** — Feature importance, residual diagnostics, and data quality checks.
- **[About](about.qmd)** — Methods, data sources, and project background.
